text:
The article focuses on the working mechanisms and structural developments of the Jordanian administrative judiciary system and on the developments that took place in 2020. Following the amendments to the Constitution in 2011 and the issuance of the Administrative Judiciary Law No. 27 of 2014, Jordan moved from a monistic system to a pluralistic judicial system with two levels of litigation, the Administrative Court and the Supreme Administrative Court. This development has greatly contributed to enhancing the rule of law, ensuring justice and thus providing a safeguard for individuals and enhancing good governance as well as increasing citizen confidence. The number of judicial cases filed against state institutions has also significantly increased.

This study provides an analytical and empirical investigation of the Supreme Administrative Court, the Administrative Court, and the Administrative Public Prosecution through a series of statistical tables and judicial data in addition to an analysis of the annual reports provided by these bodies. The study reveals a remarkable increase in the number of judiciary decisions (judgments) during the studied period despite the challenges and obstacles faced by the judiciary as a result of the Corona-19 pandemic. In addition, the study reveals a number of administrative and geographic bottlenecks, specifically the concentration of the first instance administrative judiciary decisions in the capital Amman, in light of the scattered distribution of administrative cases all over the country. The study recommends the decentralization of judiciary decisions through establishing regional courts for administrative cases, in addition to recommending the issuance of a special law to achieve judicial independence in administrative matters. In addition, the study recommends the issuance of a special law on judicial independence of administrative matters in light of the complexity of administrative cases, especially in relation to specialized regulatory provisions, notably the necessity of appointing specialized judges to deal with administrative cases, due to their distinction and specificity such as administrative litigations related to public contracts, which are currently dealt with in ordinary courts. All these recommendations emphasize that Jordan has made a definite step forward, but it, however, needs more achievements and legislative and structural reforms to establish a fully specialized and accessible dual judicial system.
